coredata.dc:title: “Dandelion” magnetic bead-driven CDs@CeO2 based-multifunctional nanoplatform for tri-modal ultrasensitive detection and efficient eradication of pathogenic bacteria
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Ying
Li, Hui
Ren, Yuwei
Wang, Yang
Jiao, Rui
Zhang, Xiyan
Yu, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Xin
Cheng, Jianghua
Ye, Yingwang
coredata.dc:description: To address the challenge of foodborne pathogens, we have developed an ultra-sensitive tri-modal detection and effective pathogen eradication strategy utilizing a “dandelion” magnetic bead-driven cerium dioxide doped with carbon dots (CDs@CeO2) multifunctional nanoplatform. Aptamer (Apt) modification CDs@CeO2 specifically recognizes and binds to the outer membrane proteins of Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium). Concurrently, mannose-functionalized dandelion-like magnetic beads selectively recognize adhesins of S. typhimurium, avoiding competition with Apt for binding sites and leveraging the strong bacterial capture capability for high-sensitivity detection. The prepared CDs@CeO2 has been demonstrated to exhibit favourable fluorescence characteristics. In the presence of H2O2, CDs@CeO2 is capable of oxidising colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxide, displaying exceptional photothermal properties. CDs@CeO2 have the potential to generate •OH through a Fenton-like reaction, which could directly damage cells. Notably, coupling the photosensitizer Ce6 to CDs@CeO2 and subsequent illumination generates 1O2, which synergistically eradicates bacteria with •OH. Detection limits of the fluorescence, colorimetry, and photothermal methods were 3.85 cfu/mL, 9.44 cfu/mL, and 5.00 cfu/mL, respectively. The platform successfully achieves precise detection of S. typhimurium in actual pre-prepared food samples through fluorescence, colorimetry, and photothermal tri-modal signals, and effectively kills bacteria, achieving trace detection and source control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CeO2 doped with carbon dots
Dandelion magnetic beads
Multi-modal detection
Enhanced sterilization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of properties of multifunctional nanoplatform
Detection of S. typhimurium in pure culture
Sterilization efficiency and mechanism of the comprehensive platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development program, China (2022YFF1100702; 2022YFF1100703); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32230084; 32102104); the Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project (2022B1111040002); the Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11); and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities\uFF08Grant No.PA2024GDGP0037; PA2024GDSK0055\uFF09.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program, China (2022YFF1100702; 2022YFF1100703); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32230084; 32102104); the Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project (2022B1111040002); the Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11); and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant nos. PA2024GDGP0037; PA2024GDSK0055). The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest related to the work reported in this manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of CeO2 multi-functional nanomaterial and “Dandelion” magnetic separation platform.

Characterization and properties of Apt/CDs@CeO2 and APF. (A) Synthesis procedure of CDs@CeO2 and APF. (B) TEM image and EDS elemental (C, N, S, Ce) mapping images of CDs@CeO2, Scale bar: 20 nm. (C) XPS full scan of CDs@CeO2. The peak splitting spectra of C, N, S, Ce (D-G). (H) Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum of DMPO following incubation with CDs@CeO2 (2 mg/mL) + H2O2 (10 mM). (I) Fluorescence spectra of apt/CDs@CeO2 and CDs@CeO2.

The conditions’ optimization of the sensing platform for S. typhimurium detection. (A) Detection procedure of tri-modal sensing platform. (B) Effect of rate of CDs and CeO2 on colourimetric and fluorescent signal. Effect of concentration of Apt/CDs@CeO2 (0–4 mg/mL) on colourimetric (C) and fluorescent (D) mode detection. Effect of pH (E), temperature (F), incubation time (G) in the colourimetric mode detection. (H-I) Represents tri-modal monitoring under different conditions, (A6: APF-apt/CDs@CeO2 + 106 cfu/mL of S. typhimurium; A0: APF-apt/CDs@CeO2 + 100 cfu/mL of S. typhimurium; C6: APF-apt/CDs@CeO2 + 106 cfu/mL of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus); C0: APF-apt/CDs@CeO2 + 100 cfu/mL of S. aureus). N = 3, ***P < 0.001, compared with group A6.

Tri-modal detection of S. typhimurium under pure culture. The corresponding calibration curve and linear relation, (A–C) fluorescence modal for 100–108 cfu/mL S. typhimurium; (D–F) colourimetric modal for 100–108 cfu/mL S. typhimurium. (G, I) photothermal modal for 100–108 cfu/mL S. typhimurium. (J–L) Analysis of specificity about tri-modal detection (106 cfu/mL, N = 3).

Detection of S. typhimurium in prepared food. (A–C) Recoveries of S. typhimurium in pre-prepared chicken, prepared beef and prepared pork by the tri-modal sensing platform. (D) Detection procedure of tri-modal sensing platform on the prepared food.

Construction of S. typhimurium sterilization platform and the mechanism analysis. (A, E) Analysis of survival rate of S. typhimurium through plate counting under different conditions. (B, F) Analysis of the yield of S. typhimurium biofilm under different conditions via crystal violet staining. (C, G) Presentation of S. typhimurium through fluorescence microscope under different conditions (Live-green /Dead-red). (D) EPR spectrum of 1O2 following incubation with Ce6@CDs@CeO2 (2 mg/mL). (H) Presentation of S. typhimurium through SEM under different conditions. (I) DCFH-DA was used to stain the ROS produced in S. typhimurium. (J) The relative expression levels of antioxidant defense related genes (sodA, katE and ahpC) under different conditions. (K) Bactericidal mechanism of CeO2 multi-functional nanomaterial. (I: PBS; II: 100 mM H2O2; III: 100 mM H2O2 + 0.2 mg/mL CDs@CeO2; IV: 100 mM H2O2 + 0.2 mg/mL Apt/CDs@CeO2; V: 100 mM H2O2 + 0.2 mg/mL Ce6@CDs@CeO2 + 660 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W/cm2, 10 min)). N = 3, ns P > 0.05, ***P < 0.001, compared with group II.

head.ce:title: “Dandelion” magnetic bead-driven CDs@CeO2 based-multifunctional nanoplatform for tri-modal ultrasensitive detection and efficient eradication of pathogenic bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Animal Food Green Manufacturing and Resource Mining of Anhui Province, Engineering Research Center of Bio-Process, Ministry of Education, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Animal Food Green Manufacturing and Resource Mining of Anhui Province, Engineering Research Center of Bio-Process, Ministry of Education, Hefei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Animal Food Green Manufacturing and Resource Mining of Anhui Province, Engineering Research Center of Bio-Process, Ministry of Education, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agro-Products Processing, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Anhui Engineering Laboratory of Food Microbial Fermentation and Functional Application, Hefei 230036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agro-Products Processing, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Anhui Engineering Laboratory of Food Microbial Fermentation and Functional Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agro-Products Processing, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Anhui Engineering Laboratory of Food Microbial Fermentation and Functional Application, Hefei 230036, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Zhan and H. Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the challenge of foodborne pathogens, we have developed an ultra-sensitive tri-modal detection and effective pathogen eradication strategy utilizing a “dandelion” magnetic bead-driven cerium dioxide doped with carbon dots (CDs@CeO2) multifunctional nanoplatform. Aptamer (Apt) modification CDs@CeO2 specifically recognizes and binds to the outer membrane proteins of Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium). Concurrently, mannose-functionalized dandelion-like magnetic beads selectively recognize adhesins of S. typhimurium, avoiding competition with Apt for binding sites and leveraging the strong bacterial capture capability for high-sensitivity detection. The prepared CDs@CeO2 has been demonstrated to exhibit favourable fluorescence characteristics. In the presence of H2O2, CDs@CeO2 is capable of oxidising colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxide, displaying exceptional photothermal properties. CDs@CeO2 have the potential to generate •OH through a Fenton-like reaction, which could directly damage cells. Notably, coupling the photosensitizer Ce6 to CDs@CeO2 and subsequent illumination generates 1O2, which synergistically eradicates bacteria with •OH. Detection limits of the fluorescence, colorimetry, and photothermal methods were 3.85 cfu/mL, 9.44 cfu/mL, and 5.00 cfu/mL, respectively. The platform successfully achieves precise detection of S. typhimurium in actual pre-prepared food samples through fluorescence, colorimetry, and photothermal tri-modal signals, and effectively kills bacteria, achieving trace detection and source control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the expansion of the convenience food sector, the significance of hygienic quality in the production, manufacturing, and storage processes of prepackaged food materials had escalated [1,2]. Prepackaged food items of animal origin, including meat, poultry, and seafood products, are prevalent in the market [2–4]. Prepackaged meals may potentially lead to significant outbreaks of foodborne diseases across Asia and even globally [3]. Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), one of the predominant sources of foodborne diseases, could induce symptoms ranging from fever to bacteremia and sepsis [5]. Detection and eradication of S. typhimurium are imperative to mitigate the threat posed by foodborne pathogens [6]. Conventional detection methodologies are time-intensive and financially burdensome [7]. Existing novel detection techniques often encounter challenges related to operational complexity, reduced sensitivity, and the requirement for expensive equipment [8–10]. Additionally, the majority of nanoplatforms are predominantly focused on bacterial detection without addressing bacterial eradication, potentially delaying disease treatment or leading to secondary contamination [11]. Consequently, developing a cost-effective, straightforward platform for the early detection and source control of S. typhimurium is of paramount practical significance.

The Fenton reaction eliminates bacteria through oxidation by •OH produced by the decomposition of H2O2 [12]. Research by Liu et al. found that three-dimensional porous g-C3N4/Fe3O4 exhibits excellent antibacterial activity under visible light through a photo-Fenton reaction [13]. Nonetheless, the traditional Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle reaction is inefficient, prompting the exploration of more efficient alternatives [12]. Cerium dioxide (CeO2) nanoparticles have garnered attention for their ability to easily switch between stable valence states of Ce3+ and Ce4+ depending on environmental conditions, displaying various enzymatic activities such as peroxidase-like activity [14]. The aforementioned property renders CeO2 a strong candidate for colorimetric sensing [14,15]. It is therefore of great importance to develop a multifunctional, multi-signal internal standard correction detection platform based on CeO2 that is not affected by its macromolecular matrix or the color interference of endogenous reductants. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are employed extensively for the labeling of fluorescence signals due to their distinctive fluorescence characteristics, including photostability, multicolour emission, low cost, and good biocompatibility [16]. By doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur, the photophysical properties of CDs are significantly enhanced, making them suitable for applications in bioimaging, biosensing, and antimicrobial agents [17,18]. When CDs are doped into CeO2, the composite is conducive to forming a stable monodisperse suspension [19]. Despite their potential, the application of CDs@CeO2 in pathogen detection remains unexplored.

Antibody-functionalized magnetic beads have demonstrated effective performance in mitigating interference from food matrices. However, the high cost of recognition elements and the low binding efficiency of the beads themselves present certain limitations. Addressing sample matrix interference further, Lai et al. [20] used PEG-mediated ampicillin functionalized magnetic beads to modify the surface of magnetic nanoparticles with biological ligands, which has been shown to improve dispersion and achieve efficient bacterial capture [21]. Functionalizing magnetic beads with mannose selectively improves bacterial separation efficiency, while helping to form a stable monodisperse suspension, improving the effect of sample pretreatment [22]. Additionally, Chen et al. prepared a new type of nanoparticle by covalently attaching polyethyleneimine and hyperbranched polyether polyols to the gold shell of magnetic beads, displaying excellent stability in complex biological systems [23]. Interestingly, Chen et al. also connected bovine serum albumin and polyethene glycol 2k to magnetic beads to form dandelion-like biomimetic magnetic nanoparticles [24]. However, an excess of positively charged groups in intermediaries can damage the stability of magnetic nanoparticles [25]. Therefore, developing a stable and specific magnetic bacterial enrichment platform is an urgent research gap that needs to be addressed.

In this study, we present a novel multifunctional CeO2-based nanoplatform for nanozyme-triggered multi-signal detection of S. typhimurium. A dandelion-shaped magnetic separation platform was constructed to improve the sample pretreatment, enzyme signal amplification and pollutant treatment after detection. The experimental process is shown in Fig. 1. Initially, CDs@CeO2 is synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method, featuring high peroxidase activity and vigorous blue fluorescence intensity. At the same time, specific Apt were used as recognition elements to capture S. typhimurium. The prepared CDs@CeO2 could oxidize TMB to the blue product in the presence of H2O2, and the absorption peak of the blue product at 654 nm can be used to generate a temperature signal by laser irradiation. Notably, it was innovatively proposed that γ-polyglutamate (PGA) was used as a medium to bind 4-aminophenyl-α-D-mannopyranoside (AM) on the surface of Fe3O4 to enrich S. typhimurium in prepared food samples of animal origin [26,27]. Combining the advantages of magnetic separation platform and solid phase extraction, the CeO2-based sandwich method was further constructed to realize fast, accurate, and portable detection through colourimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal signals. After confirming positive detection results, the target bacteria were eliminated using the multi-functional CeO2 detection platform. Subsequently, the photosensitizer Ce6 was coupled to CDs@CeO2 for pollutant treatment to study its synergistic bactericidal effect was studied.

CDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal methods according to previously reported method with slight modifications [28]: 2.0 g diammonium citrate and 1.0 g cysteine hydrochloride were fully dissolved in 30 mL of ultra-pure water. After 20 min of ultrasound, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 8 h in the reaction kettle. Subsequently, the larger particles were removed from the reaction product of black liquid by centrifugation at 8000 × g for 10 min. After freeze-drying, the prepared CDs were stored at 4 °C away from light for use.

CDs@CeO2 was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method with slight modifications [19]: 0.5 g of CeO2 was dissolved into 10 mL of deionized water, and 0.3 g of CDs was added. The mixture was ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min and stirred for 2 h, followed by heating in a reaction kettle at 100 °C for 3 h. The product was then centrifuged at 8000 × g, and the precipitation was freeze-dried for 12 h.

For the synthesis of Apt/CDs@CeO2, 500 μL of CDs@CeO2 (4.0 mg/mL) was supplemented with 200 μL of Apt (10 μM) and mixture (300 μL) containing 10 mM 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 5.0 mM N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). After incubation at 180 rpm for 2.5 h, Apt/CDs@CeO2 was obtained.

Initially, 0.1 mL of 10 mg/mL Fe3O4 magnetic beads was incubated overnight with 5 % bovine serum albumin (BSA). Excess BSA was then removed magnetically. Subsequently, a mix of 10 mM EDC and 5 mM NHS was added to activate the beads’ carboxyl groups, stirring in a vertical mixer for 1 h. The activated magnetic beads were separated and dispersed by alternating magnetics with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) washing three times. Then 10 mg PGA was added and reacted in 1.0 mL PBS for 4 h, and the mixture was continuously rotated to synthesize PGA@Fe3O4. After the coupling reaction, the unreacted PGA was washed away using PBS through magnetic absorption. Subsequently, the carboxyl group on PGA were activated by the addition of 2.90 mg EDC and 3.26 mg NHS in 1 mL PBS for 2 h. AM@PGA@Fe3O4 (APF) was synthesised by adding 15 mg/mL of AM to the activated PGA@Fe3O4 precipitate for 4 h. Finally, the APF was redissolved in 1.0 mL of sterilized PBS (1.0 mg/mL) and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for subsequent use.

Dispersed 1.0 g of amino-rich CDs@CeO2 in 10 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasonication for 1 h. Added 100 µL of NHS (0.2 mg/mL) and 1.0 mL of EDC (0.3 mg/mL), and stir the mixture magnetically. Then 2.0 mg of Ce6 was added to the suspension and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 25 ℃ for 24 h. Centrifuged at 8000 × g for 15 min and discard the supernatant. The nanoparticles were washed twice with deionized water, and the obtained Ce6@CDs@CeO2 was lyophilized.
Other materials and other methods can be seen in Supplementary materials.

CDs are known for their biocompatibility, strong fluorescence, and thermal stability, and are widely used as fluorescent markers [17]. In the study, we employ a one-pot synthesis approach to incorporate CDs into CeO2, thereby creating a multifunctional nanozyme that not only exhibits enhanced fluorescence and Fenton-like activity but also forms a novel nanoplatform endowed with peroxidase mimicking capabilities, as illustrated in Fig. 2Aa.

As shown in Fig. 2B, the transmission electron microscope image indicated that CDs@CeO2 was cubic, evenly distributed, with a diameter of 25 nm and exhibiting good dispersion. The dispersion of CeO2 was increased by coupling the CDs. Additionally, the abundant surface and edge defects of the CDs generate numerous active sites, which ensure stability during the subsequent loading of high concentrations of Apt [29]. Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was carried out to further determine the elemental composition of the material. Fig. 2B showed that incorporating the elements C, N, and S into CeO2 was successfully achieved, but the diameter was reduced by half (Fig. S1). It may be due to the formation of small CDs that continuously “etch” the large cube crystals of CeO2 [30]. The oxidation state of CDs@CeO2 could be analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2C–G and Fig. S2B displayed the full XPS scan of CDs@CeO2 and the characteristic peak spectra of C, N, S, and Ce. The distinctive peaks of C1s can be divided into C-C/C-H, C-O, CO, and O-CO, located at 284.8 eV, 286.21 eV, 288.57 eV, and 290.8 eV. The characteristic peak of N1s is -NH2 at 399.53 eV, indicating that there is an abundant -NH2 group in CDs@CeO2, which can be bound by carboxyl-modified Apt through amide bonds. The characteristic peaks of S-S 2p3/2 and S-S 2p1/2 were 163.64 eV and 164.94 eV, respectively. It is well known that the spectrum of Ce3d in the 880–920 eV range was complex due to the hybridization of the O2p valence band and the Ce4f energy level [31]. As shown in Fig. 2G, the three-dimensional spectrum of Ce consisted of 10 peaks, in which Ce4+ ions were labelled as Ce3+ ions with six peaks (898.16 eV, etc) and four peaks (885.08 eV, etc), accounting for 94.77 % and 5.23 %, respectively. The findings demonstrate that CeO2 is primarily present as Ce4+. Consequently, the coexistence and transition of CeO2 in different valence states facilitate the formation of oxygen vacancies [32]. Furthermore, the direct participation of oxygen vacancy will be anticipated to significantly improve the photocatalytic performance in subsequent bactericidal experiments [31]. From the O1s spectrum shown in Fig. S2B, it could be seen that the spectrum was asymmetric and could be deconvolved into two peaks, indicating that there are two types of oxygen. The peaks at 529.1 eV and 531.35 eV may belong to the lattice oxygenes O2- and O2 2- or OH-. The total crystallinity of CDs@CeO2 (Fig. S2C) was investigated by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD). All diffraction peaks correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) planes, which are pure fluorspar cubic structure cerium oxide (JCPDS 34-0394). Seeking to verify the surface functional groups of CDs@CeO2 again, the results of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis are shown in Fig. S2D. In the FT-IR spectrum, the absorption band at 1566 cm−1 belongs to free amino group (- NH2), and the absorption band at 1402 cm−1 is C-N stretching vibration, indicating that the CDs@CeO2 surface has more abundant -NH2 groups [33].

To investigate the catalytic mechanism of CDs@CeO2 for H2O2, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was employed as a marker, and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was utilized. The ESR spectrum exhibited four signal peaks with intensity ratios of 1:2:2:1 (Fig. 2H), confirming that •OH are the primary products in the catalytic process. Consequently, when TMB was added to the CDs@CeO2 and H2O2 system, the nanozyme catalyzed the formation of •OH from H2O2, which converted the colorless TMB into the blue product. Furthermore, to evaluate the affinity of CDs@CeO2 for the substrates, the Michaelis constant (K m) was used as a parameter. A lower K m value indicates a higher affinity of the enzyme for the substrate. Using the Michaelis-Menten equation, the K m value was determined to be 0.234 mM for TMB and 0.235 mM for H2O2, as depicted in Fig. S3. The K m values for CDs@CeO2 are within an acceptable range and even lower than those reported for other nanozymes in previous studies [34,35]. In conclusion, CDs@CeO2 exhibits a high affinity for H2O2 and TMB, indicating that CDs@CeO2 exhibits a high peroxidase activity. Additionally, after dispersing in deionized water for 20 days, both fluorescence and peroxidase activity of CDs@CeO2 were found to be stable (Fig. S4).

To verify the successful functionalization of CDs@CeO2, the fluorescence spectra of CDs@CeO2 and Apt/CDs@CeO2 were measured. The results demonstrated a blue shift of 5.0 nm in the fluorescence spectrum of Apt/CDs@CeO2 relative to CDs@CeO2(Fig. 2I), indicating a change in surface polarity after coupling with the aptamer. Furthermore, the zeta potential of Apt/CDs@CeO2 was more negative than that of CDs@CeO2 (Fig. S5), further confirming the successful modification of CDs@CeO2 by the aptamer. Multi-signal sensing based on nanozymes has attracted significant attention due to its simplicity, low cost, and the portability of the required equipment [36]. Most of the colourimetric sensing based on nanozymes currently available are interfered by matrices such as protein and fats in the actual samples, which eventually leads to negative bias in the detection results [37]. To overcome these limitations, the Apt/CDs@CeO2 multifunctional nanozyme probe was combined with a magnetic capture probe to achieve quantitative analysis. In other words, mannose functionalization on the surface of magnetic beads can effectively improve the efficiency of bacterial separation [22,26]. Additionally, the elongation and load capacity of magnetic beads can be significantly increased by medium coupling, resulting in a stable and specific magnetic bacterial enrichment platform [21,27].

In this study, we constructed a dandelion-shaped magnetic separation platform to improve sample pretreatment, enzyme-catalyzed signal amplification, and post-detection contaminant treatment. As shown in Fig. 2Ab, PGA is coupled to a carboxylated magnetic bead by a carbodiimide reaction. The carboxyl groups on PGA were activated again, and the amino group on AM were coupled to prepare. After chemical modification, the mean zeta potential varied from − 21.3 mV (Fe3O4) to − 27.9 mV (PGA- Fe3O4) and − 28.1 mV (AM@PGA@Fe3O4, APF), respectively (Figs. S6A, B). These results indicated an increase in the hydration layer and the change of the groups on the surface of the magnetic beads, demonstrating the successful coupling of PGA and AM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of properties of multifunctional nanoplatform
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the detection performance of the multifunctional nanozyme platform, the experimental conditions for fluorescence, colourimetric, and photothermal signal output were optimized. The ratio of CDs to CeO2, Apt concentration, Apt/CDs@CeO2 concentration, BSA concentration, AM concentration, incubation pH, incubation temperature and incubation time were important factors affecting the three signalling molecules.

The ratio of CDs to CeO2, varying from 1: 1 to 5: 1, was investigated to ensure the superior properties of CDs@CeO2 ( Fig. 3B). The results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@CeO2 increased in accordance with a gradual increase in the occupancy of CDs. Subsequent weakening may be attributed to the fluorescence aggregation quenching of CDs; Additionally, the catalase activity of CDs@CeO2 exhibited a gradual decline with an increase in the percentage of CDs, which may be due to the fact that CDs occupied a certain amount of oxygen vacancy, resulting in a decrease in the enzyme activity of CeO2. In subsequent studies, 3: 1 with the highest signal-to-noise ratio was selected as the optimal concentration. Aiming to obtain a stable temperature signal, the irradiation time of NIR 660 of the blue reactant was subsequently optimized. As shown in Fig. S7A, the signal reached stability when the irradiation time was 240 s, so the irradiation time of 240 s was selected. Simultaneously, the effects of Apt and Apt/CDs@CeO2 concentrations were investigated, with their concentrations ranging from 0 to 2 µM and 0–4 mg/mL, respectively. Subsequent studies identified 2 µM and 2 mg/mL as the optimal concentrations for Apt and Apt/CDs@CeO2, respectively (Figs. S7B–C, Fig. 3C–D; Fig. S7D). As shown in Fig. S8A, when the target is present, 0.5 % BSA encapsulation reduces the non-specific adsorption of magnetic beads, thereby increasing the strain catch. Next, the amount of AM was investigated based on strain capture, and the results of the Supplementary Fig. S8B showed that the peak value was reached at 15 mg/mL.

Aiming to optimise the overall characteristics of the multifunctional nanozyme and dandelion magnetic trapping platform, a typical color reaction was performed using TMB as substrate (Fig. 3A). In general, the catalytic activity of nanozyme is mainly dependent on the reaction conditions [38,39]. This study investigated the effects of reaction time, the amount of nanozyme, buffer pH, and temperature on the TMB color reaction catalyzed by the oxidase-like Mn3O4/C. As shown in Fig. 3E–G, with the increase of reaction time, the absorbance at 654 nm gradually increased, and the equilibrium and stable state were reached after 20 min. Therefore, the TMB oxidation time was set to 20 min. When the buffer pH ranges from 3.0 to 8.0, the platform exhibits varying enzymatic activities. The catalytic activity peaks at a pH of 3.0, however, the strong acid environment was not conducive to fluorescence emission; hence, pH 5.0 was selected for subsequent reactions. Proposed platform has good catalytic activity over a wide temperature range of 25–45℃. For the convenience of operation, 37℃ was chosen for subsequent experiments. To explore signal efficiency, the feasibility of the integrated platform was tested under optimal conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3H and J, the signal value of group A6 were significantly higher than that of other components, indicating that the ligand sensor has high sensitivity to S. typhimurium. Since the detection of fluorescent signals in the precipitates was weak, we chose supernatants with significant differences for signal monitoring, as shown in Fig. 3I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. typhimurium in pure culture
ce:section.ce:para: Apt can target the recognition of S. typhimurium outer membrane proteins (OMPs), while mannose can selectively recognize adhesin (type I Fim H) on the surface of S. typhimurium [40]. In the study, Apt-modified multifunctional nanozyme was combined with mannose-functionalized dandelion magnetic beads, and S. typhimurium was used as the detection object to verify the fluorescence, colourimetric, and photothermal signal output of the three-mode platform (Fig. 1B). To maximize the detection performance of the platform, we optimized the conditions mentioned above. Subsequently, we performed fluorescence analysis. The supernatant was collected after the last magnetic separation to determine the fluorescence intensity. The results show that the fluorescence intensity (F) of Apt/CDs@CeO2 is proportional to the logarithm of S. typhimurium concentration (Lg C) in the range of 3.30 × 101–3.30 × 107 cfu/mL. In this case, we propose a linear equation of F = -0.049 Lg C + 1.05 with coefficient of determination R 2 = 0.976 ( Fig. 4A–C). Meanwhile, the formula 3σ/slope calculation (σ corresponds to the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements [41,42]) gives a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.85 cfu/mL. The precipitate was dissolved by adding 200 μL buffer and TMB and H2O2 after taking 50 μL. We observed that the enhanced absorbance of the blue product at 654 nm was related to the concentration of S. typhimurium (Fig. 4D–F). The results show that this absorbance (A) enhancement is proportional to the logarithm of S. typhimurium concentration (Lg C) between 3.30 × 101 and 3.30 × 107 cfu/mL. The fitted linear regression graph is A = 0.076 Lg C + 0.41, coefficient of determination R 2 = 0.991, similarly, the LOD is 9.44 cfu/mL. Finally, the photothermal performance is temperature detection using a 660 nm near-infrared laser to rapidly illuminate blue product. S. typhimurium concentration (Lg C) dependent increase in temperature (T) was also visually observed on a handheld thermal imager (Fig. 4G–I). By fitting temperature and S. typhimurium concentration, a linear relationship of T = 4.77 Lg C + 25.15 (R 2 = 0.985) was established in the photothermal analysis, and LOD was 5.00 cfu/mL. Compared with the analytical sensitivity of other reported methods [43–45], the established dual-recognition platform has comparable or even higher accuracy and ideal detection performance for S. typhimurium detection.

To ensure the accuracy of the results, specific analyses were performed under the same conditions for E. coli, C. sakazakii, L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and S. typhimurium using the nanoplatform we developed. Fig. 4J–L shows that the intensity of fluorescence, UV absorption and temperature induced by S. typhimurium changes significantly. In contrast, the signal intensity of other strains changes little, indicating that the platform has good selectivity. Consequently, we have developed a typhoid-specific, tri-modal sensor with significant potential for pathogen detection. This sensor is suitable for practical applications due to its ability to selectively identify target bacterial strains in complex environments.

To further validate the practicability and accuracy of the platform, we manually spiked each sample with three concentrations of S. typhimurium to evaluate the analytical capability of the proposed three-mode detection platform driven by a dual-identification strategy under optimal conditions. The developed three-mode induction sensor is used for quantitative detection. As shown in Fig. 5A–C, the recovery rate of the three-pattern recognition platform is 80–110 %, which was sufficient to demonstrate that the strategy shows a good recovery rate. Furthermore, the standard deviation of the analytical model for S. typhimurium is less than 10 %, demonstrating robust quantitative capabilities of the strategy (Table S1). Lastly, a comparison of our study’s test results with those obtained using the standard plate count method revealed that the results were consistent between the two sets of results. Notably, the platform takes only 60 min, is easy to operate, and has a three-mode signal. Compared with relevant literature reports in recent years [46,47], the multi-modal sensor constructed in the study has a lower detection limit, wider linear range and faster response time, providing a new idea for designing a high-precision, high-selectivity and high-reliability early-warning detection method for microbial contamination of food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sterilization efficiency and mechanism of the comprehensive platform
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigation into the germicidal capabilities and mechanisms of the integrated platform has been conducted, offering a foundation for the development of demand-driven, precision-targeted sterilization strategies. As shown in Fig. 6A–C, through plate counting, crystal violet staining, and SYTO-9/PI staining, it can be seen that CDs@CeO2 and Apt/CDs@CeO2 have particular bactericidal activity when mixed with H2O2, and the bactericidal ability of Apt-modified is significantly enhanced compared with CDs@CeO2. This may be due to the fact that Apt modification narrowed the distance between the strain and the nanozyme, forming a concentrated reactive oxygen species (ROS) atmosphere in a small range (Fig. 6A). As can be seen from Fig. 6A–C, in addition to eliminating planktonic bacteria, they also have a strong destructive capability against the biofilm of S. typhimurium. We hypothesize that this is due to the peroxidase activity of the multifunctional nanozyme CDs@CeO2, which promotes the production of •OH thus causes minor damage to bacteria [41]. The main bactericidal method of the ligand sensor is that the peroxidase catalyzes H2O2 to produce toxic •OH.

Recently, photocatalytic kinetics has attracted great interest in sterilization [48]. Ce6 is the most widely used commercial photosensitizer and has excellent anti-plaque biofilm ability [49]. It produces singlet oxygen mainly through a type II action mechanism of photocatalytic kinetics. Compared with traditional photosensitizers, such as methylene blue, it has the characteristics of high singlet oxygen yield and low toxicity [50]. Here, we expect to achieve in situ deactivation of bacteria based on the dual dynamic properties of Ce6@CDs@CeO2 chemical dynamic and photocatalytic kinetics, to realize the purpose of on-demand sterilization. To verify the successful loading of Ce6, EPR experiments showed that the above multifunctional nanozyme system could produce 1O2 (Fig. 6D). Meanwhile, its bactericidal ability and biofilm elimination ability were much higher than those of CDs@CeO2 (Fig. 6E, and G).

In view of further exploring the bactericidal mechanism of the above materials, we conducted Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis on the biofilm post-sterilization. The results indicated significant morphological changes in the bacteria within the deposits (Fig. 6H), including shrinkage, deformation, and bacterial rupture. Then, the ROS content generated in the system was analyzed, as shown in Fig. 6I. The results showed that CDs@CeO2, Apt/CDs@CeO2, and Ce6@CDs@CeO2, when mixed with H2O2, respectively, could increase ROS in the system and achieve bactericidal effect, which was similar to the study of Pan et al. [35]. At the same time, seeking to study the expression of oxidative stress-related proteins in the strain itself, we analyzed A and B proteins by RT-qPCR. The results of Fig. 6J revealed that after S. typhimurium was treated with CDs@CeO2, Apt/CDs@CeO2, and Ce6@CDs@CeO2 mixed with H2O2, respectively, the expression levels of genes related to bacterial oxidative stress response were up-regulated. Compared with groups III and IV, the relative expressions of superoxide dismutase, catalase-related genes and alkyl catalase reductase-related genes sodA, katE and ahpC in group V were more significantly up-regulated (p < 0.001). This may be due to the synergistic bactericidal action of photocatalytic kinetics and chemical kinetics, inducing S. typhimurium to produce a violent oxidative stress response [51]. Therefore, the above indicated that the multifunctional nanozyme could kill the strain by destroying the cell structure and enhancing the oxidative stress of the strain, as shown in Fig. 6K.

In summary, we have developed a new method utilizing “dandelion” magnetic beads to drive CDs@CeO2 for tri-modal precise detection and efficient antibacterial applications. The dandelion magnetic beads, based on a mannose affinity strategy, enhance the dispersibility of the beads and significantly improve bacterial separation efficiency. Moreover, the “dandelion” magnetic beads facilitate convenient nanozyme labeling and elution while simultaneously achieving tri-modal precise detection and efficient antibacterial applications for S. typhimurium through a sandwich strategy. In addition, the total detection time of the biosensor is less than 60 min, while the system also provides signal cross-validation, improving accuracy and reliability. Using S. typhimurium as a model, we have demonstrated the high sensitivity and specificity of the method, making the platform a promising platform for detecting S. typhimurium. Furthermore, the updated recognition components allow for the detection of multiple foodborne pathogens, paving the way for the design and development of high-performance nanomaterials for broad-spectrum pathogen detection. In the study, dandelion magnetic bead-driven CDs@CeO2 multifunctional nanoplatform meets the demands for high sensitivity and high precision in various scenarios, demonstrating strong potential for detection and bactericidal action. However, the methods still need improvement, and the integration of artificial intelligence technology will enhance complete high-throughput detection, which is expected to be applied to field applications in food safety monitoring and clinical diagnosis.

Ying Zhan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing − original draft, Analysis. Hui Li: Data curation, Validation. Yuwei Ren: Review & editing. Yang Wang: Review. Rui Jiao: Validation. Xiyan Zhang: Conceptualization. Xiaoyu Yu: Software. Xin Zhang: Investigaton. Jianghua Cheng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yingwang Ye: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program, China (2022YFF1100702; 2022YFF1100703); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32230084; 32102104); the Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project (2022B1111040002); the Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11); and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant nos. PA2024GDGP0037; PA2024GDSK0055).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest related to the work reported in this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136903.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size-controllable Fe-N/C single-atom nanozyme with exceptional oxidase-like activity for sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase
Sens. Actuators B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Chen, S. Li, Y. Liu, X. Zhang, Y. Tang, H. Chai, et al., Size-controllable Fe-N/C single-atom nanozyme with exceptional oxidase-like activity for sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase, Sensor Actuat B-Chem, 305(2020) 127511.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A concise detection strategy of Staphylococcus aureus using N-succinyl-chitosan-dopped bacteria-imprinted composite film and AIE fluorescence sensor
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Guo, Y. Zheng, Y. Liu, X. Feng, Q. Dong, J. Li, et al., A concise detection strategy of Staphylococcus aureus using N-Succinyl-Chitosan-dopped bacteria-imprinted composite film and AIE fluorescence sensor, J Hazard Mater, 423(2022) 126934.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-S. Liu, X. Wei, X. Zhao, L.-J. Chen, X.-P. Yan, Near-infrared photothermal/photodynamic-in-one agents integrated with a guanidinium-based covalent organic framework for intelligent targeted imaging-guided precision chemo/PTT/PDT sterilization, ACS Appl Mater Inter, 13(2021) 27895-27903.

Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Gao, D. Huang, Y. Deng, W. Yu, Q. Jin, J. Ji, et al., Chlorin e6 (Ce6)-loaded supramolecular polypeptide micelles with enhanced photodynamic therapy effect against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Chem Eng J, 417(2021) 129334.

Aggregate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, S. Xu, Q. Lyu, Y. Huang, W. Wang, Ce6 nanoassemblies: Molecular mechanism and strategies for combinational anticancer therapy, Aggregate, 5(2023) e443.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ROS defense systems and terminal oxidases in bacteria
Antioxidants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.B. Borisov, S.A. Siletsky, M.R. Nastasi, E. Forte, ROS defense systems and terminal oxidases in bacteria, Antioxidants, 10(2021) 839.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ying Zhan: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2023 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Hui Li: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2022 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Yuwei Ren: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2021 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Yang Wang: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2020 till now. Research interests: food safety analysis.
Rui Jiao: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2021 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Xiyan Zhang: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Doctor degree from 2021 till now. Research interests: food safety analysis.
Xiaoyu Yu: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Master’s degree from 2023 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.
Xin Zhang: Studied in Hefei University of Technology for Master’s degree from 2023 till now. Research interests: the synthesis of nanomaterials and food safety analysis.

Jianghua Cheng: Researcher, Institute of Agro-Products Processing, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Anhui Engineering Laboratory of Food Microbial Fermentation and Functional Application. Research interests: microbial fermentation and functional application.
Yingwang Ye: Professor, Doctor's tutor, Hefei University of Technology. Research interests: food quality and safety analysis.
